title: SEGMENT 16 — Adenovirus for CRISPR delivery: fitting Cas9 plus guide plus donor template

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Adenovirus for CRISPR delivery: fitting Cas9 plus guide plus donor template
One of the unexpectedly important applications of adenoviral vectors in the last decade has been the delivery of CRISPR-Cas9 components for genome editing.
And this application leverages exactly the property that distinguishes adenovirus from AAV — the cargo capacity.
CRISPR-Cas9, as you may know, has three main components for most editing applications.
Cas9 itself, the DNA-cutting enzyme, which is a fairly large protein — the most-used variant, from Streptococcus pyogenes, has a coding sequence of about 4.1 kb.
The guide RNA, which targets Cas9 to a specific DNA sequence, only about 100 base pairs.

And, for editing applications that involve repair templates — homology-directed repair or knockin of new sequences — a donor template, which can range from a few hundred base pairs for small edits to several kilobases for inserting full new genes.
Add it all up.
Cas9 plus guide plus a substantial donor template easily exceeds AAV's 4.7 kb limit.
You can squeeze a Cas9 expression cassette and a guide cassette into a single AAV vector — barely — by using a compact promoter and removing all non-essential elements.
But there's essentially no room left for a donor template.
So if you want to deliver a full edit-and-replace CRISPR system in a single vector, AAV is too small.
Adenovirus solves this directly.
First-generation adenoviral vectors can carry Cas9, guide, and a meaningful donor template — say, 6 to 7 kb total — with room to spare.
Gutless adenovirus can carry vastly more, including entire genes as donor templates for large knock-ins.
The cargo capacity advantage of adenovirus, which is mostly a footnote when comparing vaccine platforms, becomes the deciding factor when comparing CRISPR delivery options.
There are some specific advantages of adenovirus for CRISPR delivery beyond just cargo capacity.
One: transient expression is actually a feature for CRISPR.
You want Cas9 active long enough to make the edit, then gone.

Sustained Cas9 expression in cells you have already edited is a recipe for off-target editing — Cas9 keeps cutting at other places in the genome, with low probability per unit time, and over months that adds up.
Adenovirus's natural transient expression profile, often described as a disadvantage for gene therapy, is exactly right for CRISPR.
Two: high expression levels.
Adenovirus drives strong expression of its payload in transduced cells.
Strong Cas9 expression maximizes editing efficiency.
Combined with the transient profile, you get a pulse of high Cas9 activity followed by clearance — almost ideal kinetics for editing.
Three: ability to deliver multiple components in a single vector.
Some CRISPR strategies require multiple guides, or guide-plus-Cas9-plus-donor, or even base editor or prime editor systems that are larger than ordinary Cas9.
The cargo capacity of adenovirus accommodates all of these.
The trade-off is the immune response.
Adenoviral delivery of CRISPR components, especially in vivo, brings all the immunology we have already discussed.

This has limited adenoviral CRISPR delivery mostly to ex vivo applications — where you transduce cells in a dish, edit them, and transplant the edited cells back into the patient — or to localized in vivo applications where the immune response is tolerable.
For applications like CAR-T cell editing, where cells are manipulated in a controlled ex vivo setting, adenoviral CRISPR delivery has been quite useful.
The big idea here: when you have a delivery problem that's bottlenecked on cargo size, adenovirus is your friend, and CRISPR has been one of the most important examples of that.

The same large carrying capacity that justified gutless vectors for delivering huge dystrophin transgenes also justifies first-generation adenoviral vectors for delivering complete CRISPR editing systems.
The vector finds its niche where the alternative is too small.
